point_001: The vote-k algorithm lacks sufficient ablation studies; in particular, it is unclear how important the model confidence 'stratification' step is, and whether a simpler baseline like k-means clustering on the similarity graph would perform comparably.
point_002: The relationship between vote-k and standard clustering algorithms (e.g., k-means) is not discussed, making it hard to assess the true novelty and advantage of the proposed graph-based approach.
point_003: The unlabeled pool U is drawn from pre-existing curated/labeled datasets in all experiments, raising concerns about whether vote-k would remain effective on truly uncurated or randomly collected corpora.
point_004: There is no analysis of label distribution in the annotated subset selected by vote-k; since selection is based purely on input similarity, the resulting label distribution could be skewed, and it is unclear whether performance gains are uniform across classes or concentrated on well-represented ones.
point_005: The choice of k=150 for vote-k is applied uniformly across all tasks without justification, even though the optimal k likely depends on the size and nature of each task's unlabeled pool.
point_006: Algorithm 1 has an initialization ambiguity: when L is empty, all node scores are zero, and it is unclear how the argmax is resolved deterministically and whether this initial choice affects the final selected set.
point_007: There is a discrepancy between the prose description of vote-k (suggesting 9M/10 examples are selected via confidence scores) and Algorithm 1 (lines 11–16 appear unrelated to confidence scores), creating confusion about the actual procedure.
point_008: The cost of encoding all unlabeled instances with Sentence-BERT is not accounted for when comparing methods, potentially understating the computational overhead of vote-k.
point_009: The diversity and representativeness metrics in the appendix show only small differences between Random and vote-k, and no qualitative analysis (e.g., example showcases or t-SNE visualizations) is provided to give intuition about what vote-k actually selects.
point_010: The paper does not explain why vote-k combined with similarity-based retrieval works well, but vote-k alone with random example selection performs similarly to random selection overall—a deeper analysis of this interaction is needed.
point_011: Important baselines are missing: (a) a confidence-score-only selection strategy without the graph component, and (b) soft prompt-tuning methods as an alternative few-shot approach.
point_012: The direct comparison between fine-tuned RoBERTa-large and much larger models like DaVinci-002 in Figure 2 and the abstract is potentially misleading, given that the authors themselves state this is not intended as a head-to-head comparison.
point_013: The paper does not explore whether vote-k and similarity-based retrieval remain beneficial when combined with chain-of-thought prompting, which is a widely adopted and relevant technique for in-context learning.
point_014: The experimental design uses an inconsistent set of (dataset, model) combinations across tables, making it difficult for future work to build directly on these results with a consistent benchmark.
